title: Microwave-driven Floquet-Fano interference in a ring-chord quantum dot structure for enhanced spin-caloritronic performance
abstract: We investigate photon-assisted thermoelectric transport in four--quantum-dot nanostructures featuring ring and ring--chord geometries coupled to ferromagnetic leads. Focusing on the interplay between microwave-induced Floquet sidebands and geometry-driven Fano interference, we employ the nonequilibrium Green's function formalism combined with Floquet theory and a self-consistent Hartree treatment of electron-electron interactions within the linear response regime. The inclusion of a longitudinal interdot chord bridging the lead-coupled dots introduces a discrete interference pathway that competes with the continuum of ring-mediated states, giving rise to pronounced Fano resonances. Microwave irradiation dynamically reshapes these resonances through photon absorption and emission processes, enabling tunable control of electrical conductance, thermopower, and electronic thermal conductance. Quantitatively, at an intermediate temperature of $T=0.3Γ_0$ (where $Γ_0$ denotes the dot-lead coupling strength), the microwave-driven ring-chord geometry exhibits an exceptional thermoelectric figure of merit of ZT $\approx$ 12 and an optimal efficiency--power trade-off, reaching nearly $62\%$ of the Carnot efficiency with an output power of $6.24~\mathrm{fW}$. Crucially, the combination of spin-polarized injection from the leads and Zeeman splitting within the dots induces a robust spin-dependence within this Floquet--Fano interference. This cooperative interplay results in an enhanced spin Seebeck response and a maximum spin thermoelectric figure of merit of nearly $Z_sT \approx 18$. Our findings establish microwave-driven engineering of Fano interference as an effective strategy for modulating spin-caloritronic behavior in multi-quantum-dot devices.
summary: This study demonstrates that combining microwave-driven Floquet engineering with Fano interference in a ring-chord quantum dot structure can significantly boost thermoelectric efficiency and spin-dependent transport. By using geometry to suppress thermal conductance while using microwaves to tune energy-selective channels, the authors achieve remarkably high figures of merit. This provides a strategy for designing high-performance nanoscale heat engines and spin filters.
main_problem: Investigating how the interplay between microwave-driven Floquet sidebands and geometry-driven Fano interference can be used to enhance the thermoelectric and spin-caloritronic performance of multi-quantum-dot nanostructures.
main_result: The ring-chord geometry under microwave driving achieves an exceptional thermoelectric figure of merit ZT approx 12 and a spin figure of merit ZsT approx 18, reaching nearly 62% of the Carnot efficiency.
method: Nonequilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism combined with Floquet theory and a self-consistent Hartree treatment of electron-electron interactions.
model_or_system: A four-quantum-dot nanostructure in two geometries (pure ring and ring-chord) coupled to ferromagnetic leads, featuring on-site Coulomb interaction, Zeeman splitting, and time-periodic microwave driving.
key_observables: Electrical conductance, thermopower (Seebeck coefficient), electronic thermal conductance, spin-dependent transport, thermoelectric figures of merit (ZT and ZsT), and efficiency at maximum power.
important_parameters: Microwave amplitude, frequency, temperature, dot-lead coupling strength, spin polarization, and Zeeman splitting.
main_assumptions: Wide-band approximation for lead self-energies, linear response regime, and neglect of phonon heat transport.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of ring and ring-chord geometries; Figures 3-4 plot conductance, thermal conductance, and Seebeck coefficient vs. energy; Figures 5-8 show temperature and power-efficiency trade-off dependencies; Figures 9-12 illustrate spin-dependent conductance, thermopurity, and parameter space contour plots.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the system geometry, details the Floquet-NEGF theoretical framework, compares transport in pure ring vs. ring-chord geometries, analyzes the impact of microwave driving on thermoelectric efficiency, and concludes with a study of spin-caloritronic performance under magnetic fields.
why_interesting: The paper explores the dynamic control of quantum interference and energy-selective transport using periodic driving, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and Floquet engineering of many-body states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the four–quantum-dot nanostructure coupled to ferromagnetic electrodes: (a) Pure ring geometry, where four quantum dots form a closed loop and are connected to the left and right leads through dots 1 and 3 with spin-dependent tunneling rates ΓLσ and ΓRσ, respectively. (b) Ring–chord geometry, where an additional direct coupling (chord) between dots 1 and 3 introduces an alternative transport pathway.

FIG. 2. Zero-microwave transmission T (E) as a function of energy for the four–quantum-dot system, where the black curve corresponds to the closed ring geometry (t13 = 0) and the red curve to the ring–chord configuration (t13 ̸= 0), show- ing geometry-induced Fano interference. Inset: Magnified view of the central energy window E ∈[0.5, 2.5] highlighting the asymmetric line shape and antiresonance-like suppression of transmission in the ring–chord case.

FIG. 3. Electrical conductance G in panels (a) and (b), and thermal conductance κ in panels (c) and (d), plotted as func- tions of the normalized on-site energy εi/Γ0 for both undriven and driven cases at two different temperatures. Panels (a) and (c) correspond to the pure ring configuration, whereas panels (b) and (d) represent the ring–chord geometry.

FIG. 4. Seebeck coefficient S [(a),(b)] and figure of merit ZT [(c),(d)] versus the normalized on-site energy εi/Γ0 for both undriven and driven cases at two different temperatures. Panels (a),(c) show the pure ring and (b),(d) shows the ring– chord geometry.
FIG. 5. Temperature dependence of the maximum thermo- electric figure of merit for the (a) pure ring and (b) ring–chord geometries in the undriven (∆d = 0) and microwave-driven (∆d = 0.2) regimes.
